free_text: Cross sections and thermonuclear reaction rates for
51V(alpha,n)54Mn and 51V(alpha,p)54Cr.
The University of Melbourne 5U Pelletron
accelerator
Three targets of 99.95% pure natural vanadium metal
were prepared, two on gold backings of stopping
thickness for 10 MeV alpha-particles, and the other
a self-supporting transmission target. The backed
targets were prepared by evaporation of metallic V
from previously outgassed tungsten boats on to gold
substrates 0.13 mm thick. The adopted values for the
thickness were (2.17+-0.11) x 10**18 and (4.18+-
0.21) x 10**18 51V atoms/cm. The transmission target
was prepared by evaporation of V on to a glass slide
coated with 99.999% pure cesium bromide.
LVL-NUMB changed on E-LVL in sub.004,005
SD: Small changes in all Subents.
Data in Subent 006, 007 were restored from the table.
A 125 cm**3 Ge(Li) detector located 0.8 cm
from the target
Scale error. The main contributors being
* detection efficiency 2%,
* target thickness 5%,
* beam current integration 4%.
Statistical error

The target was mounted in a stainless-steel
cross located at the centre of a 32 x 32 x 44 cm**3
paraffin castle containing six 3He-filled proportional
counters, all feeding a common amplifier.
neutron-counting measurements
Scale error. The main contributors being
* detection efficiency 12%,
* target thickness 5%,
* beam current integration 4%.
Statistical error

DATA-ERR -> ERR-S.
The transmission target mounted in a stainless-steel
chamber 60 cm in diameter and maintained at a pressure
Faraday cup
The telescope consisted of a transmission
detector, thick enough to stop 10 MeV alpha-particles
but thin enough to transmit all protons of energies
for which statistical-model calculations predicted a
significant yield, and a surface-barrier detector
with a depletion depth sufficient to stop all protons
of interest.
Scale error. The main contributors being
* the uncertainty in the channel number defining the
low-energy limit of the alpha-particle scattering
peak 3%,
* angular-distribution corrections 8%,
* the accuracy to which the alpha-particle scattering
was known to follow the Rutherford law 2%.
Statistical error
Fig.5 from Nucl.Phys.A551(1993)158
